coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation on incorporating wire mesh into phase change material-based battery packs at elevated ambient temperatures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ganji, Mohammad J.
Agelin-Chaab, Martin
Rosen, Marc A.
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a novel partial copper mesh integration in a phase change material (PCM)-based battery pack for nine series-connected 21700 lithium-ion cells (5000 mAh). The pack’s thermal behavior was investigated experimentally at discharge rates of 1C–6C (5 A–30 A) and ambient temperatures of 22 °C, 32 °C, and 42 °C. The novelty lies in embedding a partial copper wire mesh directly within the PCM. In addition, this research addresses scenarios where the ambient temperature reaches the PCM’s melting point, enabling evaluation of both positive and negative aspects of the ambient environment. Furthermore, a governing thermodynamics equation is developed, enabling quantification of PCM latent heat utilization by integrating experimentally measured heat generation, sensible energy absorption, and convective heat dissipation. This framework allows for a more accurate and theoretically grounded determination of the PCM melting fraction under varying thermal conditions. Results demonstrated improved thermal conductivity and a 40% average increase in heat dissipation at 22°C. At moderate discharge rates (1C–2C), the mesh-PCM configuration eliminated latent heat utilization and extended safe operation up to 3C (15 A). Under 5C–6C at moderate ambient temperatures, cell temperatures stabilized around 60 °C. Although elevated ambient temperatures introduce heat inflow, the mesh-PCM configuration transitioned faster to positive heat transfer compared to the pure PCM case. Nevertheless, at 1C and 42 °C, the mesh performed worse, transferring more heat from the hot environment into the system, highlighting the trade-off in increasing thermal conductivity. Overall, the partial mesh integration substantially enhances battery thermal management by reducing peak temperatures and enhancing heat dissipation without altering the airflow cooling system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery thermal management system
Phase change material
Copper wire mesh
Ambient temperature
Experimental analysis
Li-ion cylindrical cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Temperature gradient between PCM and copper pipes
Analysis of heat transfer dynamics and energy balance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), grant number RGPIN-2018-05369.The authors acknowledge with gratitude the financial support provided by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (especially the NSERC CREATE and NSERC Discovery Grant programs). The technical support of Seham Shahid, a recent graduate of Ontario Tech, is also greatly appreciated.

The authors acknowledge with gratitude the financial support provided by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (especially the NSERC CREATE and NSERC Discovery Grant programs). The technical support of Seham Shahid, a recent graduate of Ontario Tech , is also greatly appreciated.
This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), grant number RGPIN-2018-05369 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup. (a) Isometric view of the battery pack configuration, (b) a close-up image of the copper mesh, (c) mesh configuration and location of top and bottom thermocouples, and (d) a top view of the battery pack showing the thermocouple placement.
Actual image of the experimental setup, including the battery pack inside the thermal chamber.
Schematic of the data acquisition and electrical setup.
Paraffin 40–42 PCM temperature profiles of melting and solidification (time axis is normalized for better visualization of the temperature profile).
Discharge power usage profile of the battery modules.
INR21700-50S rate of heat generation and total generated heat at various discharge rates.
Maximum temperatures recorded by the (a) top thermocouples and (b) bottom thermocouples, under varying discharge rates and ambient temperatures.
Maximum temperature differences recorded by (a) top thermocouples, and (b) bottom thermocouples.
Heat dissipation rates through copper pipes and total heat generation rate under discharge rates of (a) 1C, (b) 2C, (c) 3C, (d) 4C, (e) 5C, and (f) 6C.
Total heat transfer analysis across discharge rates and ambient temperatures, showing heat generation, heat dissipation, and sensible energy.
Physical properties of materials used in experiment. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The physical properties provided are measured at 25 °C and 1 bar [22,36–38].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The PCM used in this study is 40–42 paraffin wax.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total discharge times for each discharge rate.
Total error and maximum uncertainty of the experiments and measured parameters.
PCM latent energy utilization percentage across various ambient temperatures and discharge rates.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation on incorporating wire mesh into phase change material-based battery packs at elevated ambient temperatures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University, Oshawa, Ontario L1G 0C5, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science, Ontario Tech University Oshawa, Ontario L1G 0C5, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a novel partial copper mesh integration in a phase change material (PCM)-based battery pack for nine series-connected 21700 lithium-ion cells (5000 mAh). The pack’s thermal behavior was investigated experimentally at discharge rates of 1C–6C (5 A–30 A) and ambient temperatures of 22 °C, 32 °C, and 42 °C. The novelty lies in embedding a partial copper wire mesh directly within the PCM. In addition, this research addresses scenarios where the ambient temperature reaches the PCM’s melting point, enabling evaluation of both positive and negative aspects of the ambient environment. Furthermore, a governing thermodynamics equation is developed, enabling quantification of PCM latent heat utilization by integrating experimentally measured heat generation, sensible energy absorption, and convective heat dissipation. This framework allows for a more accurate and theoretically grounded determination of the PCM melting fraction under varying thermal conditions. Results demonstrated improved thermal conductivity and a 40% average increase in heat dissipation at 22°C. At moderate discharge rates (1C–2C), the mesh-PCM configuration eliminated latent heat utilization and extended safe operation up to 3C (15 A). Under 5C–6C at moderate ambient temperatures, cell temperatures stabilized around 60 °C. Although elevated ambient temperatures introduce heat inflow, the mesh-PCM configuration transitioned faster to positive heat transfer compared to the pure PCM case. Nevertheless, at 1C and 42 °C, the mesh performed worse, transferring more heat from the hot environment into the system, highlighting the trade-off in increasing thermal conductivity. Overall, the partial mesh integration substantially enhances battery thermal management by reducing peak temperatures and enhancing heat dissipation without altering the airflow cooling system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Maximum capacity of PCM’s latent energy (J)
PCM melting fraction (PCM latent heat utilization percentage) (%)
Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as the dominant energy storage technology for electric and hybrid vehicles due to their superior performance characteristics, including high specific energy, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rates [1,2]. However, the thermal management of LIBs remains a critical challenge, particularly under high discharge rates and elevated ambient temperatures. The heat generated during the charging and discharging can lead to significant safety concerns, such as thermal runaway, and reduced operational efficiency. Excessive temperatures degrade the battery's capacity and pose risks of thermal runaway and fire hazards [3,4]. Therefore, effective thermal management systems are essential to maintain the battery temperature within the optimal operational range of 20–40°C.

Furthermore, inconsistent temperature distribution and inadequate heat dissipation within battery packs can adversely affect LIB performance and longevity. Overheating accelerates degradation mechanisms and reduces the battery's lifespan. Recent developments in battery thermal management systems (BTMSs) have focused on minimizing the maximum temperature across cells and enhancing temperature uniformity within the BTMS, all while reducing the energy consumption required for cooling [5–8]. BTMSs can be broadly classified into active and passive systems based on energy usage and operational mechanisms.

Active cooling systems utilize external energy sources to facilitate heat dissipation through heat exchangers inside or outside the battery pack. These systems often involve forced convection using fluids such as air or liquid coolants to remove excess heat. While effective, active systems can be complex, heavy, and energy-intensive, potentially offsetting some of the efficiency gains of the battery system [9–11].

In contrast, passive cooling methods rely on natural heat transfer mechanisms without requiring an external energy input. Among passive methods, phase change materials (PCMs) have gained significant attention due to their ability to absorb large amounts of heat during the phase transition from solid to liquid, owing to their high latent heat of fusion. PCM-based BTMS can be integrated into electric vehicles to maintain battery temperatures below the PCM's melting point. However, a significant limitation of PCMs is their inherently low thermal conductivity, which can hinder effective heat transfer, especially once the PCM has fully melted [12]. Ganji et al. [12] demonstrated a 98% of PCM latent utilization around the 18650 cells by only applying a discharge current of 7.5A. After melting, the PCM can act as a thermal barrier between the heat-generating batteries and the cooling source, potentially leading to elevated temperatures and reduced thermal management efficiency.

Addressing the low thermal conductivity of PCMs, various strategies have been explored in the literature to enhance heat transfer within PCM-based systems. These include the incorporation of high-conductivity nanoparticles [13–15], the use of metallic foams [16,17], the integration of fins [18,19], the application of metallic wire meshes [18,20–22], and PCM encapsulation techniques [23,24]. Nanoparticles, such as silver, carbon fiber, and graphene, offer high thermal conductivity. However, adding nanoparticles can be costly and may pose challenges in achieving uniform dispersion within the PCM. Furthermore, retrieving and recycling the original PCM becomes difficult when nanoparticles are incorporated [13–15]. The use of fins is a conventional method to increase overall thermal conductivity. However, fins can obstruct natural convection within the melted PCM and create temperature non-uniformities in the melted regions [18,19]. Moreover, fins consume excessive volume and weight to cover the optimal areas. On the other hand, metallic foams and wire meshes offer a balance between enhancing thermal conductivity and increasing the total weight of the system [16,22]. Metallic wire meshes are particularly advantageous due to their cost-effectiveness, ease of production, customizability, and manufacturing consistency compared to metallic foams. However, the porosity of the foam or mesh within the chamber is an important factor. High porosity can result in insufficient thermal conductivity enhancement, while low porosity reduces the PCM volume and its latent heat capacity [16]. Moreover, previous research has demonstrated that implementing a partial metallic wire mesh within the PCM is more efficient regarding thermal performance and porosity than using a full-coverage metallic wire mesh [25]. This efficiency is attributed to achieving an optimal balance between the PCM's latent heat and the enhanced conduction provided by the mesh. The partial wire mesh allows effective heat transfer without significantly reducing the PCM volume or obstructing natural convection currents. However, this finding needs to be reevaluated for battery thermal management applications and a broader range of operating conditions. The present work, therefore, extends the partial-mesh concept to an actively discharging 21700-cell pack and assesses additional metrics, including temperature uniformity and latent-heat utilization under dynamic ambient conditions.

Several studies have investigated the integration of conductive structures and materials within PCMs to enhance thermal management. Kadam and Kongi [26], for example, added graphene nanoparticles to paraffin, while Jiang et al. [27] integrated Al2O3 into paraffin wax. Khetib et al. [28] used graphene nanoparticles with Calcium Chloride Hexahydrate-based PCM. Wang et al. [29] evaluated expandable graphite mixed with paraffin in a battery pack under various initial and ambient temperatures, concluding that temperature regulation improved with higher ambient temperatures and discharge rates. Li et al. [30] introduced an innovative passive thermal regulator using n-eicosane PCM with aluminum nitride powder, which featured a natural thermal regulation mechanism that enhanced heat dissipation as the temperature rose.

Additionally, Isfahani et al. [31] proposed a system incorporating aluminum metal foam with PCM and microchannels, demonstrating that while metal foam PCM alone minimized temperature variations, it increased maximum temperatures compared to only microchannels. Integrating both components, their hybrid approach effectively reduced temperature differentials and peak temperatures. Li et al. [32] examined the effects of incorporating copper metal foam in a PCM-based BTMS, finding that this combination achieved the lowest maximum temperatures and most uniform temperature distributions compared to pure PCM and active cooling methods. The study revealed that decreasing the porosity of the copper foam and increasing its pore density led to a reduction in the surface temperature of the batteries, highlighting the importance of optimizing foam characteristics. Lee et al. [33] found that positioning 92 % porosity copper foam PCM at the top and pure PCM at the bottom of an enclosure provided enhanced thermal management during intense heat generation scenarios, such as 3C charging, increasing the PCM's melting time delay by 50–80 s compared to pure PCM. Khaboshan et al. [34] investigated various combinations of cooling techniques, including PCMs, metal foam, and fins. Combining PCM with metal foam showed a two-fold enhancement in the melting time compared to fins and a 60 % increase in melting time compared to pure PCM. Further analysis elucidated that the metal foam facilitated uniform heat distribution, optimizing heat transfer within the system. However, these studies primarily utilized full-volume metallic matrices, which, while improving heat distribution, introduced trade-offs such as reduced latent capacity and altered convection patterns (according to Ganji et al. [25]). The current study extends this line of research by experimentally evaluating a partial wire mesh structure under high discharge rates and elevated ambient temperatures in a multi-cell cylindrical configuration.

Azizi et al. [35] demonstrated that aluminum wire mesh plates within the PCM lower the maximum temperature significantly and enhance temperature uniformity relative to pure PCM. This underscores the effectiveness of metallic wire meshes in enhancing thermal conductivity without significantly compromising the PCM's latent heat capacity. Wu et al. [21] evaluated a composite encapsulated PCM incorporating a thin layer of copper mesh. The copper mesh was sandwiched and hot-compacted between two composite PCM blocks. The results demonstrated that the maximum temperature reduction increased from 5.4 °C to 7.2 °C upon including copper mesh within the composite PCM.

In this study, a partial copper wire mesh with specified height, porosity, and number of layers is integrated directly into the PCM and positioned in direct contact with high-capacity 21700 cylindrical cells. The selection of a partial mesh, rather than a nonporous metallic interface, is intended to enhance overall thermal conductivity while preserving the beneficial effects of natural convection within the PCM. This design seeks to balance the reduction in latent heat capacity, arising from the volume occupied by the mesh, with the improvement in heat dissipation, as reported in the literature [25]. Furthermore, high-capacity 21700 cylindrical cells have been selected to facilitate higher discharge currents and longer discharge durations, thereby generating sufficient heat to investigate full PCM melting and its subsequent post-melting behavior.

Unlike prior studies that primarily estimated PCM melting fraction based on average or localized temperature readings, this study employs a comprehensive energy balance framework that integrates measured heat generation, convective dissipation, and sensible energy storage to quantify phase change dynamics and latent heat utilization. Despite extensive research on PCM-based battery thermal management systems, a key gap remains in understanding their behavior when the PCM begins to melt, and the cell temperature exceeds the PCM’s melting point. Additionally, few studies have investigated the direct incorporation of a partial wire mesh into the PCM in battery applications. In particular, there is a lack of knowledge about how mesh influences system performance during the melting operation. In addition, there is a lack of experimental investigation of the thermal performance of the mesh-enhanced PCM, particularly in battery applications. Specifically, there is limited insight into how severe discharge rates (up to 6C, 30A) and high ambient temperatures (exceeding the PCM’s melting range) affect thermal performance when the PCM’s latent heat is substantially and locally diminished. The novelty of this research lies in addressing these gaps by embedding a partial copper wire mesh directly into the PCM-based battery pack, thereby improving the overall thermal conductivity while retaining some convective properties of the melted PCM. This research offers a novel approach to improving PCM-based battery packs through partial mesh integration, ultimately yielding more robust, efficient, and reliable thermal management solutions. Through experimental investigation of temperature gradients and heat transfer under various operational conditions, the study also clarifies how mesh placement influences heat dissipation and temperature regulation once the PCM’s latent capacity begins to deplete. Although this study is primarily focused on elevated ambient temperatures to examine PCM melting and high thermal loads, it is recognized that thermal management behavior under low ambient conditions is also a critical consideration. In particular, the presence of a high-conductivity structure such as a copper mesh is expected to accelerate heat losses in cold environments, potentially resulting in battery temperatures falling below optimal operational thresholds. To address this, the integration of an adaptive active cooling/heating system is recommended to maintain thermal balance under varying ambient conditions.

This section details the experimental framework used to investigate the thermal performance of the mesh-enhanced PCM battery pack. It describes the physical components of the experimental setup, the experimental procedure for charging and discharging, the governing equations used for thermal analysis, and the uncertainty analysis performed to validate the results.

This study employed nine cylindrical lithium-ion batteries (Samsung INR21700-50S), each with a capacity of 5000 mAh and a nominal voltage of 3.7 V. The batteries were arranged in series within a cuboid battery pack with internal dimensions of 8.4 cm in width and 6.0 cm in height. These dimensions were selected to accommodate the batteries and allow external electrical connections. To connect the battery cells in series, copper strips (4 cm × 5 mm × 0.5 mm) were used due to their high electrical conductivity to minimize the heat generation from electrical resistance. Traditional nickel tabs would generate approximately four times more heat, potentially introducing measurement errors. A designated opening was created to facilitate the routing of copper strip connections, which was subsequently sealed using high-temperature, room-temperature-vulcanizing (RTV) silicone. This sealant acted as a thermal insulator, preventing unwanted convective heat transfer. Any heat generation from these strips was calculated and found to be minimal compared to the total heat production by the cells. Details of this calculation are shown in Appendix A.

The battery pack was constructed using a medium-density fiberboard (MDF) with a thickness of 6.5 mm, assembled with high-temperature-resistant sealant glue. MDF was selected for its availability and suitability for precise laser cutting. The batteries were spaced 6 mm apart, consistent with configurations used in previous research by the authors [12].

Four vertical copper air pipes with an outer diameter of 6 mm and a wall thickness of 1 mm were installed between the battery cells to facilitate heat dissipation. An air duct, matching the length and width of the battery chamber and with a height of 1.9 cm, was placed above the battery pack to dissipate heat from the copper pipes. To implement a minimal and realistic active augmentation strategy, an axial AC fan (GDSTIME 1203, rated power <1.2 W) was employed to induce a steady airflow through the duct, achieving an inlet speed of 2.1 ± 0.1 m/s, as measured by a Proster anemometer. The inlet air temperature was controlled using a 227-liter (8 cubic feet) thermal chamber (SD-508, Associated Environmental Systems) with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C. This hybrid configuration integrates the passive PCM–mesh system with minimal active support to reflect realistic operating conditions while maintaining low energy consumption. The fan-assisted airflow modestly enhances convective heat removal without dominating the thermal response, thereby preserving the passive character of the BTMS. The walls of the battery pack were assumed to be adiabatic, implying that all heat dissipation relied exclusively on the air duct. Isometric visualization of the battery pack is presented in Fig. 1 (a), illustrating the section view of the air duct and the chamber, and the proposed location of the partial copper mesh.

A square copper wire mesh with a #10 mesh number, an opening size of 1.91 mm (0.075″), and a wire diameter of 0.64 mm (0.025″) was used. A close-up image of the copper mesh is presented in Fig. 1(b). The dimensions were chosen to be consistent with a previous study by the first author [25], where the optimized mesh had a porosity of 93 % and a height of 64 % of the total enclosure height. However, the previous study focused on melting time and latent heat capacity and did not involve an analysis of thermal management for batteries or thermal uniformity. Therefore, the optimal configuration needs to be reevaluated in future research based on the current thermophysical setup. The mesh porosity ( ε ) was calculated as follows [25]:
(1)
Mesh
Porosity
ε = 1 -
Volume
of
the
mesh
Volume
of
the
Chamber
where the volume of the chamber is equal to the volume of the cuboid battery pack minus the volume occupied by the batteries. To achieve a porosity of 93 %, a height of 63.5 %, and the specified PCM volume, four layers of mesh, each with a height of 38 mm, were arranged in a grid configuration, as illustrated in Fig. 1(c). The mesh was arranged with alternating lengths of 22 mm for the interior sections and 25 mm for the exterior sections. This configuration resulted in a mesh volume of 14 ± 0.5 mL, yielding a porosity of 93 %.

To effectively monitor the temperature distribution within the battery pack, Omega 5TC-TT-T-24-72 T-type thermocouples with a reported accuracy of ±0.5 °C were incorporated. Eight T-type thermocouples were attached to the batteries, with their top-view alignment shown in Fig. 1(d). Three of them were attached at two-thirds of the battery height (where there is no partial mesh), and five were attached at one-third of the total height (where the partial mesh is present), as depicted in Fig. 1(c). These thermocouples are referred to as the top and bottom thermocouples, respectively, in the subsequent analysis found in the results and conclusion sections. This setup enables the evaluation of partial mesh effectiveness compared to full mesh configurations. An additional thermocouple was attached to the interior wall of one pipe to capture the temperature at the midpoint of the heat transfer direction, enabling estimation of the system’s total heat dissipation.

Each thermocouple was connected to an Adafruit 3263 temperature sensor (reported accuracy of ±0.075 °C) and interfaced with an Arduino Mega microcontroller (Elegoo MEGA 2560 R3). The temperature readings were transmitted to a computer via the Arduino terminal connection for real-time monitoring and data logging. As detailed in the uncertainty analysis section, the measurement error was calculated to be less than ±0.51 °C. A real image of the experimental setup is provided in Fig. 2 .

A heat flux sensor (FluxTeq PHFS-01) with an accuracy of ±0.03 µV/(W/m2) was also employed to measure the surface heat flux of one cell, providing critical data for assessing heat generation during the discharge tests. Heat flux data were recorded using a data acquisition device (NI-9211) coupled with NI-CDAQ-9178 chassis and LabView software. Schematics of the experimental setup are provided in Fig. 3 .

Paraffin wax, with a melting temperature range of 40–42 °C, was selected as the working PCM. The manufacturer's temperature specifications for the PCM were verified through a series of thermal experiments conducted both prior to and following the study to ensure its thermophysical stability. The average results of the melting experiments are depicted in Fig. 4 , confirming a melting temperature of 41 ± 1 °C. For clarity in comparative analysis, the x-axis has been normalized with time. As expected, the solidification process takes approximately twice as long as the melting process. Based on the dimensions of the battery pack and the available volume for PCM storage, 211 ± 1 mL of molten paraffin was poured into the battery pack, corresponding to 163 ± 1 g of material. The PCM volume was measured using a precision graduated syringe, with repeated measurements performed to ensure consistency and accuracy. The physical properties of the materials used in this study are provided in Table 1 .

All the experiments were conducted with the battery pack initially conditioned at 22 ± 0.5 °C. Therefore, any variation in ambient temperature solely affected the inlet airflow temperature of the duct during the discharge. Three ambient temperatures, 22 °C, 32 °C, and 42 °C, were investigated, each maintained with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C, as specified by the thermal chamber’s specifications.

The batteries were arranged in series and subjected to a standard two-stage charging protocol. First, a constant current (CC) of 4 ± 0.01 A was applied until the total voltage reached 37.8 ± 0.005 V, equivalent to 4.2 V per cell, using a DC power supply (OWON SP6103) with specified accuracy. Subsequently, a constant voltage (CV) phase at 37.8 ± 0.005 V was imposed until the current declined to 0.1 ± 0.01 A. This current was prescribed as the cutoff current in accordance with the manufacturer’s specifications.

During discharge, the series-connected batteries were operated under constant-current loads at six different rates (1C–6C), corresponding to 5 A, 10 A, 15 A, 20 A, 25 A, and 30 ± 0.01 A, respectively. These loads were imposed using a multifunctional electronic load (TDI RBL488) with specified accuracy. The discharge proceeded until the total voltage reached 22.5 ± 0.01 V, corresponding to 2.5 V per cell. A subsequent constant-voltage discharge phase at 22.5 ± 0.01 V was then maintained until the current decreased to 0.1 ± 0.01 A (cutoff current).

Fig. 5 presents the power usage profile of the battery modules during discharge, where the horizontal axis is normalized to the total discharge time for each current rate to enable direct comparison. A summary of total discharge durations for the different current rates is provided in Table 2 . The extra 4–7 min reported in Table 2 correspond to the second stage of the discharge protocol, where the batteries are discharged at a constant voltage of 22.5 V.

To quantitatively analyze the thermal data gathered during the discharge experiments, a global heat balance was applied to the system. It is assumed that all heat dissipation from the battery pack occurs primarily through the air duct, while the battery pack walls are thermally insulated. Under these assumptions, the global heat balance for the experimental setup is governed by:
(2)
Q G
=
Q D
+
Q S
+
Q L
+ ξ
where
Q G
is the total heat generated,
Q D
is the heat dissipated via copper pipes,
Q S
is the utilized sensible energy,
Q L
is the utilized latent energy, and ξ is the error term. Each term in this equation was quantified using the methods described below.

Q G
was quantified using data from the heat flux sensor. The measured heat flux was integrated over the entire discharge duration, assuming uniform heat generation across all nine cells. Fig. 6 illustrates the total rate of heat generation per cell at various discharge rates, alongside a table summarizing the total generated heat. The x-axis in Fig. 6 is normalized to facilitate clearer visualization and comparison among different discharge conditions. Further details regarding the heat generation measurement methodology and sensor calibration are provided in Appendix B.

Heat dissipated through the copper pipes (
Q D
) was calculated using convective heat transfer principles based on the Nusselt number under laminar flow conditions. The rate of heat dissipation is expressed as:
(3)
Rate
of
Heat
Dissipation
Q D
̇
= 4 h
A pipe
T pipe
-
T inlet
here, h denotes the convective heat transfer coefficient between the copper pipes and the flowing air,
A
pipe
the lateral surface area of each copper pipe available for heat exchange,
T
pipe
the temperature of the copper pipe, and
T
air
the temperature of the incoming air. The multiplier of 4 accounts for the four copper pipes utilized in the experimental setup. Integrating this equation over the discharge period provided the total heat dissipated. The detailed methodology of heat dissipation measurement is provided in Appendix C.

Sensible heat (
Q S
) refers to the energy required to raise the temperature of the PCM, excluding the energy required for phase transition. It was calculated using the specific heat capacity of the PCM (
C p
), the mass of the PCM(
m
PCM
), and the change in temperature from the initial condition to the final measured state (
T f
-
T i
). By assuming that thermal energy variations within the PCM have a negligible impact on the overall system performance, Equation (4) is approximated by taking the average of the final temperatures recorded by all thermocouples (
T f
¯
) and by treating
∫ d m
as the total PCM mass (
m
PCM
).
(4)
Q S
= ∫
C p
T f
-
T i
d m ≈
m
PCM
C p
T f
¯
-
T i

The utilized latent heat (
Q L
) was consequently determined by subtracting the total heat dissipated (
Q D
) and the sensible heat (
Q S
) from the total heat generated (
Q G
):
(5)
Q L
=
Q G
-
(
Q D
+
Q S
)

The maximum available latent heat (
Q
L , m a x
) was calculated based on the PCM mass and its latent heat of fusion, providing a reference for evaluating PCM utilization or PCM’s melting fraction (39.81 kJ). To assess the performance of the PCM in utilizing its latent heat, the melting fraction ( β ) was determined as the ratio of utilized latent heat (
Q L
) to the maximum available latent heat (
Q
L , m a x
):
(6)
β =
Q L
Q
L , max
× 100

This energy balance framework provides a quantitative approach for assessing the thermal management system’s performance. It demonstrates how effectively the PCM and copper mesh arrangement contribute to regulating battery temperatures and managing heat across various discharge conditions and ambient conditions.

Uncertainty quantification in this study followed the methodology proposed by Moffat (1988) [40], which distinguishes between bias errors and precision errors. Bias errors refer to systematic offsets inherent to the measurement system, calculated to approximately ±0.51 °C and ±2–40 W/m2 in temperature and heat flux measurements, respectively. Precision errors denote random fluctuations observed in repeated measurements, calculated to a maximum ±0.26 °C and ±10 W/m2 in temperature and heat flux measurements, respectively.

All experiments presented in this study were repeated at least three times to ensure repeatability and reliability. Additionally, selected tests were repeated again at the conclusion of approximately 120 charge/discharge cycles to assess potential impacts from extended thermal cycling and battery aging. These final repeated experiments demonstrated negligible variations (<0.8 %) in temperature profiles, confirming consistent thermal and structural performance throughout extensive cycling.

Table 3 summarizes the minimum and maximum temperatures and heat fluxes measured, with their associated bias errors, maximum precision errors, and maximum total uncertainties. Further details regarding the error calculations are provided in Appendix D.

The combined analysis of bias error, precision error, and total uncertainty ensures the reliability and accuracy of the experimental results, supporting both the validity of the measurements and the overall experimental methodology.

This section presents the experimental results and a detailed analysis of the thermal performance of the battery pack with and without the integrated copper mesh. The discussion is structured to first evaluate the system's effectiveness in temperature control—a key objective of any BTMS—by analyzing maximum temperatures and temperature uniformity. Subsequently, it delves into the underlying heat transfer mechanisms, using a governing energy balance equation to quantitatively assess heat dissipation, sensible energy storage, and latent heat utilization. This approach connects the observed thermal behavior to its fundamental physical drivers.

Fig. 1(c) and (d) illustrate the placement of the top and bottom thermocouples within the battery pack. Their respective maximum average temperatures are presented in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). Under an ambient temperature of 22 °C, incorporating the copper mesh consistently reduces the maximum temperature in both the top and bottom sections of the chamber, with more pronounced reductions observed in the bottom region. For instance, at 6C (the highest manufacturer-approved discharge rate for the specified cell), the bottom maximum temperature is reduced by approximately 8.9 °C, whereas the top temperature is reduced by 4.4 °C.

However, at higher ambient temperatures, the top thermocouples in the mesh-PCM configuration sometimes record temperatures similar to or exceeding those observed in the pure PCM arrangement (up to 1.7 °C higher). In contrast, the bottom thermocouples consistently show thermal benefit from the mesh, exhibiting notable temperature reductions even under elevated ambient conditions (averaging 3.9 °C, up to 8.9 °C). These observations suggest that extending the mesh coverage to the entire chamber in future designs is expected to yield more uniform and effective thermal control throughout the system.

At the highest discharge rate (6C, 30A), considering the significantly shorter discharge duration (∼14 min), integrating the copper mesh effectively lowers the maximum temperature in the bottom region by approximately 7–9 °C, notably keeping it below 60 °C under standard ambient conditions. In the top section, where mesh coverage is absent, temperature reductions range from 0.1 °C to 4.4 °C at 6C, underscoring the limited impact of partial mesh coverage under higher thermal loads.

In comparison, the pure PCM configuration shows a steady increase in peak temperatures with rising discharge rates. At standard ambient temperature, the critical temperature threshold of 60 °C is exceeded when transitioning from 4C to 5C discharge. In contrast, the mesh-PCM configuration completes the full 6C discharge while maintaining the maximum temperature below 60 °C in the bottom domain. Whereas the pure PCM system ultimately reaches a maximum temperature of 67.5 °C at 6C under normal ambient conditions (22 °C).

Moreover, the mesh-PCM configuration effectively maintains the temperature below the PCM’s melting point at discharge rates of 3C and below, whereas pure PCM achieves comparable performance only at 2C and below. These results demonstrate that mesh integration enables higher-rate discharge operation while improving thermal regulation and safety margins.

While reducing the peak temperature is a primary goal, ensuring temperature uniformity across the pack is equally critical for battery health, preventing localized degradation and extending cycle life. An effective BTMS must not only cool the cells but also minimize thermal gradients. Therefore, the maximum temperature differences recorded by the thermocouples, which serve as a key indicator of thermal uniformity within the PCM domain, are analyzed next.

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) quantify the temperature uniformity by depicting the maximum temperature difference observed during the discharge trials. This value represents the highest instantaneous temperature gradient measured across the monitored points on the cell surfaces. It is determined by calculating the difference between the hottest and coldest thermocouple readings at each measurement step, and then identifying the peak of these differences over the full discharge duration. A smaller maximum temperature difference signifies a more uniform thermal distribution. The analysis is presented separately for the top and bottom thermocouple groups to compare thermal uniformity in regions with and without the integrated mesh. Although the mesh lowers the overall maximum temperature, it also increases nonuniformity, nearly doubling the temperature difference along the thermocouples at higher discharge rates. In contrast, configurations using pure PCM exhibit maximum temperature differences under 2 °C at the top and under 3 °C at the bottom. In the mesh-enhanced designs, these differences exceed 4 °C at 5C and 6C discharge rates.

These trends highlight the performance of each configuration under high thermal stress. The pure PCM system maintains relatively consistent gradients even at elevated discharge rates, although at the cost of higher absolute temperatures (as shown in Fig. 7). Meanwhile, the mesh-PCM setup, while effectively limiting peak temperatures, introduces sharper thermal gradients at higher discharge loads.

The non-uniformity in temperature distribution can be attributed to several factors. First, the partial coverage of the mesh, particularly in the top section, leaves some areas without enhanced heat transfer, leading to localized temperature elevations. Moreover, the mesh’s 93 % porosity, while advantageous for PCM circulation, inherently limits its conduction contact area. Finally, the current vertical arrangement and layering of the mesh create a thermal conduction pathway in the vertical direction but obstruct natural convection paths within the melted PCM, particularly in the horizontal direction. This interference contributes to horizontal temperature gradients and localized nonuniformities. To overcome this limitation, future designs could explore horizontal or combined horizontal–vertical mesh layouts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature gradient between PCM and copper pipes
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 9 illustrates the temperature gradient (
Δ T
) between the battery surfaces and the copper pipes for different discharge rates, defined by:
(7)
Δ T =
T
average
-
T
pipe
where
T
average
is the average surface temperature of the batteries and
T
pipe
is the copper pipe temperature. The objective is to maintain a minimal positive temperature gradient, which indicates a strong thermal connection within the PCM, ensuring effective heat transfer and cooling.

At higher ambient temperatures (32 °C and 42 °C), the system experiences a negative gradient in the early stages of 1C and 2C discharges, indicating that the pipes are initially transferring heat into the cells. As the discharge proceeds and the cells warm, the gradient becomes positive, demonstrating that the pipes eventually act as a cooling source. Integrating the copper mesh shortens this period of negative gradient and improves the overall cooling phase, especially at elevated discharge rates.

In the 3C to 6C range, the gradient magnitude increases owing to higher heat generation. Nevertheless, the mesh-enhanced PCM consistently outperforms pure PCM in maintaining a lower temperature gradient, leading to better thermal regulation.

A notable observation across different ambient temperatures (22 °C, 32 °C, and 42 °C) is that the temperature gradient trends appear similar or even slightly better at higher ambient temperatures, despite the system being subjected to a hotter environment. This can be attributed to the fact that when both the system and the ambient environment are hot, the temperature difference between the battery surface and the pipes becomes smaller, resulting in a lower observed gradient. This creates a misleading view of better performance at higher ambient temperatures. However, this observation is misleading because the smaller temperature gradient is primarily due to the elevated temperature of the cooling medium, which reduces the potential for heat transfer, rather than improved thermal conductivity. Thus, it is important to note that temperature gradients observed across different ambient conditions cannot be directly compared, and analyses should be solely based on the same ambient temperature.

The analysis of Fig. 9 highlights the effectiveness of copper mesh in improving thermal management by minimizing the initial heating effect and enhancing the cooling capability of the battery pack. The copper mesh PCM configuration performs more effectively across all discharge rates, particularly under higher thermal loads. However, caution must be exercised when interpreting temperature gradients across different ambient temperatures, as they may not accurately reflect the system’s true thermal performance. Further temperature analysis is provided in Supplementary Information A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of heat transfer dynamics and energy balance
ce:section.ce:para: The observed temperature profiles and gradients are direct consequences of the underlying heat transfer dynamics within the battery pack. To gain a deeper, quantitative understanding of how the copper mesh influences system performance, the energy balance framework developed in Section 2.3 is now applied.

During each experiment, the temperature of one of the four copper pipes was measured. Based on the methodology outlined in Section 2.3 and by applying Equation (3) to the measured pipe temperature, the rate of heat dissipation through the copper pipes was calculated and is shown in Fig. 10 . In parallel, the rate of heat generation of all nine cells under each discharge condition was also determined and plotted in Fig. 10, enabling a direct comparison between heat generation and heat dissipation.

These results indicate that, in all tested scenarios, integrating the wire mesh within the PCM enhances the heat dissipation rate through the copper pipes. In the figures, positive heat dissipation rates indicate a net cooling effect (i.e., heat is transferred from the battery cells to the copper pipes), whereas negative values signify a net heating effect (i.e., heat is transferred from the environment or the pipes to the cells). This behavior is strongly influenced by ambient temperature: at higher ambient temperatures, the copper pipes can initially transfer heat into the battery pack until thermal equilibrium is attained, after which they begin to dissipate heat. A larger positive value corresponds to improved cooling effectiveness, and a more negative value denotes diminished cooling performance.

At an ambient temperature of 22 °C, the dissipation rates are consistently positive both with and without mesh. However, at higher ambient temperatures, the mesh‐enhanced configuration achieves a positive heat dissipation rate more quickly than the configuration without mesh, highlighting the superior thermal performance provided by the wire mesh. This improvement is largely attributed to the mesh’s ability to increase effective thermal conductivity, thereby strengthening the thermal link between the cells, the PCM, and the copper pipes.

A closer look at 1C and 2C discharge rates under the ideal ambient condition of 22 °C reveals that the rate of heat dissipation in the mesh‐enhanced PCM case is sufficient to match the heat generated by the cells. In contrast, at higher discharge rates, the shortened experiment duration coupled with higher heat generation prevents the system from reaching thermal equilibrium, as there is not enough time for heat dissipation to fully offset the generated heat. Consequently, at 1C and 2C, the proposed mesh‐enhanced battery pack demonstrates robust heat dissipation and a quicker approach to thermal equilibrium. Under these specific conditions, latent heat contributions remain negligible, aligning with the conceptual framework described by Equation (2) and illustrating an optimal scenario where latent heat utilization is unnecessary.

The integration of data from Fig. 10 over time represents the total heat dissipated through the copper pipes. The total discharge times for 1C, 2C, 3C, 4C, 5C, and 6C rates are 66.7, 35.2, 25.4, 19.8, 16.4, and 14.4 min, respectively.

According to the energy balance described by Equation (2), total heat generation must balance with the sum of heat dissipation, sensible energy, and, if applicable, latent energy. Consequently, the vertical distance between the top of the combined bars (
Q D
+
Q S
) and the dashed line marking
Q G
corresponds precisely to the latent energy absorbed (
Q L
), up to the maximum available energy of
39.81 k J
. The figure also employs distinct fill patterns to differentiate between mesh-enhanced PCM (solid bars) and pure PCM (hatched bars). This graphical representation provides an immediate visual and quantitative summary of the heat transfer characteristics for all test conditions.

At an ideal ambient temperature of 22 °C and discharge rates of 1C and 2C, the mesh‐enhanced PCM provides sufficient heat dissipation to meet the total heat generation with no reliance on latent energy. By contrast, in the pure‐PCM system, even at a 1C (5 A) discharge, the latent heat becomes necessary to handle the generated heat. As the discharge rate surpasses 2C, the experiment duration shortens, and the heat generation rate spikes, causing the amount of dissipation and sensible energy to not reach the heat generation for PCM melting to occur. Moreover, the amount of heat dissipation remains approximately constant at 10 kJ and 15 kJ for pure-PCM and mesh-PCM, respectively, for all the tested discharge rates. The integration of mesh within the PCM has consistently shown to be beneficial, with an average of 40 % increase in heat dissipation.

However, at an ambient temperature of 32 °C, although a negative heat dissipation rate occurs for a short period, the overall total heat dissipation becomes positive with linear trends. It also causes the PCM to undergo melting. This is discussed in Section 3.6.

At a high ambient temperature of 42 °C, which is similar to the melting point of the current paraffin PCM (40–42 °C), the current setup contributed to heating the battery pack instead of cooling it. This outcome implies that insulating the system from the environment or shutting down the fan mechanism may have resulted in better thermal performance. Nonetheless, at only the highest discharge rate (6C), where the pure-PCM case results in almost zero total heat dissipation, the mesh-PCM resulted in approximately a positive value of 2 kJ in heat dissipation. Conversely, at the lowest discharge rate (1C), the mesh configuration shows slightly more negative dissipation than the pure‐PCM case. This is because mesh integration enhances thermal conduction between the cells and the environment, allowing more heat transfer in either direction. However, at higher rates (2C and above), the benefits of mesh become increasingly pronounced.

Overall, the analysis of Fig. 11 underscores the notable roles of discharge rates and ambient temperature in governing the battery pack performance. The system performs effectively at lower ambient temperatures (22 °C), where sufficient heat dissipation is achieved by utilizing the mesh within the PCM. However, as the ambient temperatures rise (32 °C and 42 °C), the proposed system's effectiveness diminishes, most notably at lower discharge rates. Even under these challenging conditions, the incorporation of the wire mesh consistently mitigates negative dissipation and improves total heat removal, preventing the system from becoming a net heat source. These findings highlight the mesh’s value in optimizing the thermal performance of battery packs, particularly under more severe thermal loads.

The only exceptions occur at 1C and 2C under an ambient temperature of 22 °C in the mesh-PCM configuration, where the sum of the dissipated heat and sensible energy is sufficient to fully offset the heat generated by the cells. In all other cases, an additional energy source is required to match the total heat generated.

Fig. 12 and Table 4 summarize the melting fraction β (Eq. (6)) for all tested ambient temperatures and discharge rates. The data illustrate that the PCM melting fraction increases for higher ambient temperatures or discharge rates, reflecting a greater reliance on latent heat. Notably, the Mesh‐PCM configuration generally exhibits lower or comparable melting fractions relative to Pure PCM, indicating that its enhanced heat dissipation capabilities reduce the extent to which latent energy must be utilized. More specifically, at the moderate discharge rates of 1C and 2C under an ambient temperature of 22 °C–corresponding to approximately 160 W and 300 W of electrical power, respectively– the Mesh‐PCM configuration requires no latent heat utilization. Furthermore, at 3C, 4C, and 5C (400 W, 550 W, and 700 W, respectively), the melting fraction β in the Mesh‐PCM configuration is reduced by approximately 31–36 % compared with the pure PCM arrangement.

This study experimentally investigated the thermal performance of a phase change material (PCM)-based battery pack, focusing on partial copper mesh integration for series-connected 21700 cells (5000 mAh). The battery pack was evaluated at discharge rates from 1C to 6C and at ambient temperatures of 22°C, 32°C, and 42°C. The analyses also quantified the heat generation, heat dissipation, sensible energy, and latent heat utilization of the PCM. The novelty of this work lies in incorporating a partial copper wire mesh directly within the PCM. Moreover, this study evaluates mesh effectiveness under moderate to high discharge rates and at elevated ambient temperatures similar to the PCM’s melting point. In addition, the combined experimental and analytical approaches serve to comprehensively address the core objectives of this research. The main findings are summarized as follows:
•
The copper mesh facilitated more efficient heat transfer by establishing a superior thermal link, allowing excess heat generated by the batteries to dissipate more rapidly into the surrounding environment. Enhanced thermal conductivity between the battery surface and the air duct was identified as the key driver of this improved performance.
•
Incorporating the mesh markedly reduced peak battery temperatures, keeping the cell temperature below 60°C at discharge rates up to 6C (30 A) under moderate ambient conditions.
•
While the pure PCM effectively managed temperatures only up to 2C (i.e., below its melting point), the partial mesh-PCM configuration enabled safe operation at 3C (15 A), thus extending the feasible range of discharge rates.
•
At low discharge rates (1C and 2C), the mesh-PCM system required no latent heat utilization (β = 0). Furthermore, for 3C, 4C, and 5C at 22 °C, the PCM melting fraction β decreased by 31–36% with mesh enhancement, underscoring the mesh’s role in minimizing reliance on phase change.
•
Under high current scenarios (e.g., 15 A with mesh), the percentage of latent heat utilization was comparable to that of the 5 A, pure PCM condition, reflecting the mesh’s capacity to offset elevated heat loads. Similarly, at 30 A, the mesh case matched the 20 A, pure PCM scenario at 22 °C.
•
At a moderate ambient temperature of 22 °C, integrating mesh within the PCM consistently demonstrated an average of 40% improvement in heat dissipation relative to the pure PCM systems.
•
At elevated ambient temperatures (32 °C and 42 °C), the improved thermal conductivity also facilitated undesired heat flow from the environment into the battery pack. However, the mesh-PCM arrangement consistently achieved positive (cooling) heat flux values faster than the configurations without a mesh.
•
At an ambient temperature of 42°C, the PCM was subjected to both internal heat generation and external heat inflow, without any active cooling. The results revealed that the mesh integration slightly degraded the performance at low discharge rates due to reverse heat flow but significantly improved dissipation and thermal control at higher rates. This confirms its benefit under more severe thermal loads.
•
Deploying mesh solely in the lower section of the pack introduced temperature nonuniformity across the PCM domain, likely due to the disruption of natural convection by the vertically oriented mesh. While this configuration effectively reduced the maximum temperature and PCM melting fraction with minimal volume replacement (∼4 mL, ∼2.6 kJ), future designs should investigate alternative mesh layouts to enhance thermal uniformity as well.
•
Even when drawing 15 A, the combined mesh and PCM approach managed thermal loads effectively, indicating that this design, combined with minimal active cooling, such as a simple fan and air duct system, can sustain high-power battery applications.

In conclusion, embedding a partial copper mesh within a PCM-based battery pack offers a promising route to achieving more robust, efficient, and lightweight thermal management for high-power battery applications. By lowering peak temperatures and minimizing latent heat usage, the mesh-PCM system addresses a gap in the literature concerning alternative, less complex heat dissipation mechanisms. Future research may focus on optimizing mesh parameters, such as porosity, coverage height, and orientation, to further enhance temperature uniformity and heat transfer rates, thereby ensuring reliable performance under higher discharge loads and more extreme ambient temperatures. A proposed Design of Experiment (DoE) framework outlining these optimization pathways is presented in Appendix E.

Mohammad J. Ganji: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Martin Agelin-Chaab: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Marc A. Rosen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), grant number RGPIN-2018-05369.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge with gratitude the financial support provided by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (especially the NSERC CREATE and NSERC Discovery Grant programs). The technical support of Seham Shahid, a recent graduate of Ontario Tech, is also greatly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.applthermaleng.2025.127592.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
